meaning: 爱 (ài) is the core word for love — romantic, familial, or compassionate alike. It forms 爱情 (romantic love), 可爱 ("lovable," i.e. cute), and 喜爱 (to be fond of).
note: One of the most requested characters for tattoos and calligraphy.

meaning: 和平 (hépíng) joins 和 (harmony) and 平 (calm, level) — peace between people and nations. The single character 和 alone carries the sense of harmony.
力量 (lìliàng) means strength and power, both physical and inner. The single character 力 means "force" or "effort."
勇气 (yǒngqì) combines 勇 (brave) and 气 (spirit, energy) — the courage to act in spite of fear.
智慧 (zhìhuì) is deep wisdom and insight, a step beyond mere knowledge (知识).
自由 (zìyóu) joins 自 (self) and 由 (to follow from) — to act from oneself; freedom and liberty.
幸福 (xìngfú) is deep, lasting happiness and well-being, distinct from momentary joy (快乐).
梦想 (mèngxiǎng) joins 梦 (dream) and 想 (to wish) — an aspiration or cherished dream, not the dream of sleep (梦).
希望 (xīwàng) means hope — both the feeling and the thing hoped for.
美 (měi) means beauty and the beautiful; it appears in 美丽 (beautiful) and 美好 (wonderful).
和谐 (héxié) means harmony and balance among people and things — a central ideal in Chinese thought.
忠诚 (zhōngchéng) means faithfulness and devotion, especially to people and principles.
荣誉 (róngyù) means honor and glory earned through one's deeds.
感恩 (gǎn'ēn) is heartfelt gratitude — literally "to feel kindness."
耐心 (nàixīn) joins 耐 (to endure) and 心 (heart) — patience as an enduring heart.
宁静 (níngjìng) means calm and tranquility — inner stillness.
真理 (zhēnlǐ) means truth in the deep, philosophical sense; everyday "true" is 真 (zhēn).
龙 (lóng) is the Chinese dragon — a benevolent symbol of power, luck, and the emperor, quite unlike the Western dragon.
note: Among the most popular characters for tattoos.

meaning: 虎 (hǔ) is the tiger, a symbol of courage and a guardian against evil.
凤凰 (fènghuáng) is the Chinese phoenix — a symbol of rebirth and grace, often paired with the dragon as the empress to its emperor.
狼 (láng) is the wolf, associated with loyalty to the pack and untamed independence.
莲花 (liánhuā) is the lotus — a Buddhist symbol of purity rising unstained from muddy water.
竹 (zhú) is bamboo, a symbol of resilience and integrity — it bends in the storm but does not break.
月亮 (yuèliang) is the moon; the single character 月 also means "month."
太阳 (tàiyáng) is the sun; the character 阳 alone is the "yang" of yin-yang.
水 (shuǐ) is water — one of the Five Elements (五行) of Chinese cosmology.
火 (huǒ) is fire — one of the Five Elements (五行).
山 (shān) is a mountain; the character is a pictograph of three peaks.
海 (hǎi) is the sea or ocean; it also appears in many place names.
雪 (xuě) is snow — a frequent image in classical Chinese poetry.
家 (jiā) means both family and home; the character shows a roof over a pig — the traditional household.
朋友 (péngyou) means friend; both 朋 and 友 historically meant companion.
母亲 (mǔqīn) is the formal word for mother; the affectionate form is 妈妈 (māma).
父亲 (fùqīn) is the formal word for father; the affectionate form is 爸爸 (bàba).
战士 (zhànshì) is a warrior or fighter — literally a "battle soldier."
王 (wáng) means king; it is also the single most common Chinese surname.
女王 (nǚwáng) is a reigning queen — 女 (woman) plus 王 (king).
幸运 (xìngyùn) means good luck and fortune; everyday "luck" is also 运气 (yùnqi).
成功 (chénggōng) means success — 成 (to accomplish) plus 功 (achievement).
永恒 (yǒnghéng) means eternity, the everlasting; 永 alone means "forever."
命运 (mìngyùn) means destiny and fate — 命 (life, fate) plus 运 (fortune, movement).
灵魂 (línghún) means the soul or spirit.
心 (xīn) is the heart — both the organ and the seat of emotion and mind in Chinese thought.
生命 (shēngmìng) is life in the vital sense; 生 alone means "to be born" or "to live."
精神 (jīngshén) means spirit, vigor, and mental energy.
福 (fú) means blessing and good fortune; it is the character hung — often upside-down — at Chinese New Year.
气 (qì) is qi — the vital life energy at the heart of Chinese philosophy, medicine, and martial arts.
善良 (shànliáng) means good-natured and kind-hearted — a warmth of character central to Confucian virtue.
正义 (zhèngyì) means justice and righteousness — 正 (upright) combined with 义 (moral rightness).
慈悲 (cíbēi) is the Buddhist concept of compassion — 慈 (loving-kindness) paired with 悲 (empathy for suffering).
喜悦 (xǐyuè) is a heightened, radiant joy — deeper than everyday 高兴 (gāoxìng).
宽恕 (kuānshù) means to forgive — 宽 (broad, generous) and 恕 (to excuse, overlook a fault).
纯洁 (chúnjié) means pure and unsullied — 纯 (unmixed, pure) and 洁 (clean).
信念 (xìnniàn) is personal faith and conviction — a belief held firmly in the heart.
尊严 (zūnyán) means dignity and self-respect — the inherent worth that commands respect from others.
激情 (jīqíng) is intense passion and ardor — 激 (stirred, excited) combined with 情 (feeling, sentiment).
谦逊 (qiānxùn) means modest and humble — a virtue highly prized in Confucian ethics.
沉默 (chénmò) is silence and quietude — 沉 (to sink, deep) and 默 (silent, wordless).
韧性 (rènxìng) is resilience and toughness — the quality of bending under pressure without breaking.
星 (xīng) is a star; the doubled form 星星 (xīngxing) is the everyday word for stars in the sky.
天空 (tiānkōng) means the sky and heavens — 天 (heaven, sky) and 空 (emptiness, open space).
风 (fēng) is wind; it forms 风格 (style, lit. wind pattern) and 风景 (scenery, lit. wind view).
雷 (léi) is thunder. In Chinese mythology, 雷公 (Lord of Thunder) upholds justice by punishing the wicked.
森林 (sēnlín) is forest. The character 森 is composed of three 木 (tree) radicals, visually evoking dense woodland.
大地 (dàdì) is the earth as a nurturing, maternal force — 大 (great) and 地 (ground, earth).
江 (jiāng) is a great river. It names the Yangtze (长江) and appears in countless Chinese place names and poems.
樱花 (yīnghuā) is the cherry blossom — beloved across East Asia as a symbol of the transience of beauty.
梅 (méi) is the plum blossom — a Chinese symbol of perseverance and integrity, blooming defiantly in winter cold.
鹤 (hè) is the crane — a symbol of longevity, wisdom, and elegance in Chinese painting and poetry.
雨 (yǔ) is rain — a central image in classical Chinese poetry, evoking longing, renewal, and melancholy alike.
蝴蝶 (húdié) is the butterfly. Zhuangzi dreamed he was a butterfly — and awoke wondering which was real.
鹰 (yīng) is the eagle — a symbol of keen vision, soaring ambition, and fierce independence.
春 (chūn) is spring — the first season, a symbol of new beginnings, warmth returning, and life renewed.
玉 (yù) is jade, China's most treasured stone — a symbol of virtue, purity, and nobility for over five thousand years.
金 (jīn) means gold and metal — one of the Five Elements (五行), symbolizing clarity, precision, and prosperity.
姐妹 (jiěmèi) means sisters — 姐 (elder sister) and 妹 (younger sister) together, or sisterhood more broadly.
兄弟 (xiōngdì) means brothers, and is widely used for close male friends, comrades, and sworn allies.
孩子 (háizi) is the everyday word for child. The character 孩 originally depicted the sound of a baby's laughter.
老师 (lǎoshī) is teacher — 老 (venerable, experienced) and 师 (master, expert in a craft or art).
学者 (xuézhě) is a scholar and intellectual — one who devotes their life to learning (学).
知己 (zhījǐ) is a soulmate who truly knows you — 知 (to know) and 己 (oneself). The Chinese proverb says a true 知己 is rarer than a thousand admirers.
荣耀 (róngyào) is glory and radiant honor — 荣 (to flourish, glory) and 耀 (to shine, dazzle).
胜利 (shènglì) means victory — 胜 (to win, to surpass) and 利 (benefit, advantage).
传奇 (chuánqí) means legend — a story passed down (传) that is wondrous and extraordinary (奇).
英雄 (yīngxióng) is a hero — a person of outstanding talent (英) and great courage (雄).
团结 (tuánjié) means unity and solidarity — to gather together (团) and bind fast (结).
平衡 (pínghéng) means balance and equilibrium — 平 (level, calm) and 衡 (a scale, a measure).
重生 (chóngshēng) means rebirth and renewal — 重 (again, anew) and 生 (to live, to be born).
坚持 (jiānchí) means to persevere and persist — 坚 (firm, solid) and 持 (to hold, to maintain).
无限 (wúxiàn) means infinite and boundless — 无 (none, without) and 限 (limit, boundary).
旅程 (lǚchéng) is a journey or voyage — the full arc of travel, whether literal or metaphorical.
影 (yǐng) is shadow and reflection — used poetically for a fleeting trace or image, as in 人影 (a person's silhouette).
光 (guāng) is light and radiance. It forms 阳光 (sunlight) and 荣光 (glory) — wherever there is brilliance, 光 is there.
勇敢 (yǒnggǎn) means brave and courageous — the adjective describing a person who acts despite fear.
崛起 (juéqǐ) means to rise powerfully from humble origins — as in the rise of a nation or the emergence of a great figure.
奇迹 (qíjì) is a miracle — an extraordinary (奇) achievement or event that leaves a trace in history (迹).
冒险 (màoxiǎn) means adventure and risk-taking — 冒 (to brave, to venture) and 险 (danger, peril).
道 (dào) is the Tao — the Way, the fundamental principle underlying all things. Laozi wrote: "The Tao that can be named is not the eternal Tao."
禅 (chán) is the Chinese word underlying "Zen" — a form of Buddhism that prizes direct experience and meditation over scripture.
天 (tiān) means heaven, sky, and nature — one of the three powers (三才) in Chinese cosmology, alongside earth and humanity.
觉悟 (juéwù) is the awakening of understanding — the Buddhist concept of enlightenment, literally "to awaken and perceive."
因果 (yīnguǒ) is cause (因) and effect (果) — the Buddhist law of karma expressed in classical Chinese.
缘分 (yuánfèn) is the Chinese concept of fated connection — the invisible bond, woven by fate, that draws people together.
阴 (yīn) is the yin of yin-yang — the feminine, dark, cool, and receptive principle of Chinese cosmology.
阳 (yáng) is the yang of yin-yang — the masculine, bright, warm, and active principle.
宇宙 (yǔzhòu) is the universe — 宇 (space) and 宙 (time); together they express the totality of space-time.
自然 (zìrán) means nature — literally "self-so" or "spontaneous." In Taoism, acting in accordance with nature (自然) is the highest virtue.
